query: is bobble head doll syndrome associated with hydrocephalus

retrieved_docs.title: Arachnoid Cysts -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Arachnoid cysts may cause atypical symptoms also. Bobble-head doll syndrome, a rhythmic movement of the head, has been reported due to third ventricular dilation and potentially cerebellar involvement. [32] [33] Depression that resolves with the marsupialization of an arachnoid has also occurred in some patients. The presentation of an arachnoid cyst causing depression is quite rare but plausible given the location and marked mass effect the cyst had on the brain. [34] The rupture of an arachnoid cyst resulting in a subdural hygroma has rarely been observed. The rupture can be due to trauma or can be spontaneous. Usually, the subdural hygroma can be followed without treatment, but surgical intervention is occasionally required. Furthermore, the traumatic rupture of an arachnoid cyst rarely results in hemorrhage or a subdural hematoma. [35] [36] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: An objective examination with the help of specific psychometric tests is necessary for the assessment of subcortical frontal lobe deficits. Some examples are the grooved pegboard test, the Stroop test, the digit span test, the trail-making A/B test, and the Rey auditory-verbal learning test.

retrieved_docs.title: Dysdiadochokinesia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Trauma: Head CT scan to screen for increased intracranial pressure and correlate clinical symptoms with concerning findings such as midline shift or herniation syndromes

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus had complex pathogenesis and multiple causes. There are four main types of hydrocephalus: obstructive, communicating, hypersecretory, and NPH. Hydrocephalus may be classified as congenital and acquired. Most tumors can obstruct the ventricular system and cause hydrocephalus. Removal of the tumor usually resolves the hydrocephalus. In children, hydrocephalus is often part of a genetic syndrome. The primary treatment for hydrocephalus is surgical with a ventricular shunt placement and CSF diversion. ETV and plexectomy are options in certain types of hydrocephalus. Acute hydrocephalus is an emergency.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital hydrocephalus is usually present at birth. An unusually large head is a significant sign of congenital hydrocephalus. There are many more symptoms associated with congenital hydrocephalus like tense and bulge fontanelle, a disjunction of sutures, thin and shiny scalp with prominently visible veins, stiff arms, and legs prone to contractions, “the setting sun” outlook (pupils of the eyes may be close to the lower eyelid), breathing difficulties, poor feeding, the unwillingness of the infant to bend or move their neck or head, delayed developmental stages. A “cracked pot” sound on percussion of the skull is the so-called Macewen sign.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may present with delayed growth and sexual maturity, high-pitched cry, irritability or drowsiness or both, possible vomiting and seizures. Failure of upward gaze is due to pressure on the tectal plate and is of supranuclear origin. In severe untreated forms of hydrocephalus, other elements of dorsal midbrain syndrome (Parinaud syndrome) may be seen.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation is nonspecific, subject to multiple factors, including the severity of hydrocephalus, intracranial hypertension, and associated comorbidities. [1] Most patients will present in their first year of life with signs and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure. [6] [7] [12] The most common manifestation is macrocephaly, affecting 90% to 100% of patients during their first months of life. [7] The syndromic form of DWM may also have malformations of the heart, face, limbs, and gastrointestinal or genitourinary system that could draw initial medical attention. [9] Almost 75% of cases will have hydrocephalus by the age of 3 months, and eventually, almost 90% of the patients will have hydrocephalus. A case series of 42 patients with DWM showed hydrocephaly in all patients at the time of diagnosis, vermian hypoplasia in 88%, and cerebellar hypoplasia in 59%. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Fifty percent of children with untreated hydrocephalus die before age 3, and only approximately 20% to 23% will reach adult life. Most of the patients with untreated hydrocephalus who reach adult life will have motor, visual, and auditory deficits. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventriculoperitoneal Shunt -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Dandy-Walker syndrome presents with a cystic deformity of the fourth ventricle, hypoplasia of the cerebellar vermis, and an enlarged posterior fossa

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus is the symptomatic accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) inside the cerebral ventricles. [1] This accumulation may be due to obstruction in the normal flow of the CSF, or to problems with absorption into the venous system by the Pacchionian arachnoid granulations, or due to excessive production of CSF. Dandy first describes hydrocephalus as communicating and non-communicating (obstructive) in early 1913, and since then, many more classifications were proposed. In adults, there are four different types; obstructive, communicating, hypersecretory, and normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH). Congenital or developmental hydrocephalus is often present at birth and is often part of a genetic syndrome or spinal dysraphism.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dandy-Walker malformation or syndrome is a rare congenital neurological anomaly that affects the development of the cerebellum, the region of the brain responsible for motor coordination and balance. This posterior fossa anomaly is characterized by agenesis or hypoplasia of the vermis and cystic enlargement of the fourth ventricle, causing upward displacement of the tentorium and torcula. Most patients have hydrocephalus at the time of diagnosis. Dandy-Walker malformation is the most common posterior fossa malformation, and it typically occurs sporadically. The syndrome can manifest with a wide spectrum of neurological and developmental symptoms, making timely recognition and management crucial for improving patient outcomes. Treatment consists of treating the manifestations and associated comorbidities. This activity reviews the presentation of Dandy-Walker malformation and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for patients with DWM is highly variable and dependent on several factors, including the severity of the malformation, the timeliness of diagnosis and intervention, and the presence of associated complications. In milder cases, where the cerebellar vermis is less affected and with minimal intracranial pressure, individuals may experience fewer neurological deficits and lead relatively normal lives with appropriate management and support. However, in more severe cases with significant cerebellar and posterior fossa abnormalities, patients are at a higher risk of experiencing developmental delays, intellectual disabilities, motor coordination issues, and hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: Charcot-Bouchard Aneurysm -- History and Physical -- Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Accurate measurement of vital signs, a baseline GCS score, and a complete physical exam with a structured neurological exam is required. With the worsening of cerebral edema and increased hematoma size, neurological symptoms progress within minutes to hours. The specific neurological deficits seen reflect the location of hemorrhage, such as: Putamen involvement presents with hemiparesis with depression of consciousness. Thalamic hemorrhages produce hemianesthesia more prominently than hemiparesis, along with an upward gaze. Pontine hemorrhages present with pinpoint pupils, quadriparesis, dysconjugate ocular mobility disorder, and coma. Lobar hemorrhages present with hemiparesis, hemisensory deficits, and speech impairment. Cerebellar hemorrhages present with vertigo, ataxia, and paralysis of conjugate gaze on one side.

retrieved_docs.title: Wallenberg Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The outlook for patients with Wallenberg syndrome depends on the size of the infarct. Still, most patients generally have a better outcome than other ischemic stroke syndromes, except for some lacunar syndromes. Gait instability or ataxia is the most typical sequelae. Sometimes hiccups can be intractable.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Many patients remain clinically asymptomatic for years, while others may present with a variety of comorbidities leading to earlier diagnosis. [9] Treatment is generally focused on alleviating hydrocephalus and posterior fossa symptoms, often including surgical interventions like ventriculoperitoneal and cystoperitoneal shunting. [6] [10]

The nervous system also encompasses procedures such as muscle‑nerve entrapment (إحصار عصبي عضلي) and is closely linked to neurosurgical interventions for CSF diversion. Insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts—including the intraperitoneal ventricular shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) and shunts to the nasopharynx—are essential for managing ventricular enlargement or hydrocephalus. Systemic nervous system disorders are broadly related to vascular headache, illustrating how anatomical sites, disease classifications, clinical similarities, and surgical treatments are interwoven in the comprehensive understanding and care of nervous system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1567
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt is classified as a ventriculoatrial shunt procedure, reflecting its function of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to the atrium for the management of hydrocephalus and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site” includes the specific procedure referred to in Arabic as “التحويلة البطينية البريتونية” (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). This type of shunt diverts cerebrospinal fluid from the brain’s ventricles to an extracranial location, typically the peritoneal cavity, and is employed in the management of conditions such as hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2238
retrieved_docs.content: Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a neurosurgical procedure that falls under the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. Within this category, the specific technique known as التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is identified as a child procedure, representing a particular method of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location such as the peritoneal cavity. Recognizing that Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a subset of the general shunt management interventions and that التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is a specific variant of this procedure aids clinicians in selecting and performing the appropriate surgical approach for conditions like hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 234

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a condition that manifests during the fetal period. The fetal period itself is linked to other prenatal abnormalities, including wanverhouding; foetale ascites and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal hydrocephalus causing disproportion). Recognizing that fetal disproportion associated with myelomeningocele, as well as ascites and hydrocephalus, all occur within the same developmental window underscores the importance of comprehensive fetal assessment to identify and manage multiple concurrent anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: The procedural hierarchy places the general operation “Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt” as the parent, with sub‑procedures such as Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site and its child التحويلة البطينية البريتونية. The ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx is considered synonymous or closely related to the broader extracranial ventricular shunt procedure. The nervous system also serves as the procedural site for إحصاب عصبي عضلي (neuro‑muscular blockade), reflecting the range of neurosurgical interventions that may involve both CSF diversion and neuromuscular control. retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an etiological factor for several neurological conditions. Recognizing that the same allowed qualifier can underlie these diverse disorders aids clinicians in understanding their pathogenesis, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2236
retrieved_docs.content: استسقاء الدماغ (hydrocephalus) is situated within the broader category of Brain and Nerves, indicating that it is considered a condition affecting the central nervous system. It is also identified as a member of the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term), reinforcing its classification as a neurological disorder. Clinically, استسقاء الدماغ is associated with خرف (dementia), suggesting that patients with hydrocephalus may present with or develop dementia‑related cognitive impairments. Recognizing استسقاء الدماغ’s placement within Brain and Nerves, its membership in Neurological Disorders, and its link to خرف is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and comprehensive patient care.

Both hydrocephalus and migraine are situated within the broader category of brain and nerves, underscoring their shared involvement of central nervous system structures. The term “hlava” (head) is categorized under hydrocephalus, emphasizing its cranial location. Additional hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to disseminated encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور), with the nervous system identified as the associated anatomic site. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2149
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a prenatal growth abnormality that manifests during the fetal period. In the same developmental window, other fluid‑related fetal conditions also arise, including wanverhouding with foetale ascites, wanverhouding with foetale hydrops, and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal cerebral hydrocephalus causing disproportion). These entities are all linked by their occurrence in the fetal period, reflecting a spectrum of fetal fluid accumulation and structural imbalance that can be identified through prenatal imaging and require coordinated obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2798
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a specific neurological disorder that belongs to the broader category of Brain and Nerves and is listed as a member of the “Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term)” classification. The anatomical term “hlava” (head) is also identified as a parent concept for استسقاء الدماغ, underscoring that the condition affects the cranial compartment. Clinically, استسقاء الدماغ is associated with dementia (خرف), indicating that patients with hydrocephalus may develop or exhibit cognitive decline characteristic of خرف. This integrated view highlights the hierarchical placement of استسقاء الدماغ within brain‑related disorder taxonomies and its important clinical link to خرف, which is relevant for diagnosis, monitoring, and management in patient care.
